corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: Our future work in the area will focus on investigating this duality further, using our results to improve language modeling, and strengthening the theoretical justifications of the method.
In this work, we present a generalization of language modeling to explore the graph through a stream of short random walks.
Our goal is to learn a latent representation, not only a probability distribution of node co-occurrences, and so we introduce a mapping function Φ : v ∈ V → R |V |×d .

By combining both truncated random walks and neural language models we formulate a method which satisfies all Algorithm 1 DeepWalk(G, w, d, γ, t) Input: graph G(V, E) window size w embedding size d walks per vertex γ walk length t Output: matrix of vertex representations Φ ∈ R |V |×d 1: Initialization: Sample Φ from U |V |×d 2: Build a binary Tree T from V 3: for i = 0 to γ do 4: O = Shuffle(V ) 5: for each vi ∈ O do 6: Wv i = RandomW alk(G, vi,t) 7: SkipGram(Φ, Wv i , w) 8: end for 9: end for of our desired properties.
We also present several variants of our approach and discuss their merits.
The algorithm consists of two main components; first a random walk generator and second an update procedure.

Algorithm 2 SkipGram(Φ, Wv i , w) 1: for each vj ∈ Wv i do 2: for each u k ∈ Wv i [j -w : j + w] do 3: J(Φ) = -log Pr(u k | Φ(vj)) 4: Φ = Φ -α * ∂J ∂Φ 5: end for 6: end for In the inner loop, we iterate over all the vertices of the graph.
We use the SkipGram algorithm to update these representations in accordance with our objective function in Eq.
In this section we discuss the main components of our algorithm.
Lines 3-9 in Algorithm 1 shows the core of our approach.
We present DeepWalk, a novel approach for learning latent representations of vertices in a network.
Our results show that Deep-Walk outperforms challenging baselines which are allowed a global view of the network, especially in the presence of missing information.
DeepWalk's representations can provide F1 scores up to 10% higher than competing methods when labeled data is sparse.
In some experiments, Deep-Walk's representations are able to outperform all baseline methods while using 60% less training data.
It is an online learning algorithm which builds useful incremental results, and is trivially parallelizable.
Sparsity enables the design of efficient discrete algorithms, but can make it harder to generalize in statistical learning.
In this paper we introduce deep learning (unsupervised feature learning) techniques, which have proven successful in natural language processing, into network analysis for the first time.
We develop an algorithm (DeepWalk) that learns social representations of a graph's vertices, by modeling a stream of short random walks.
DeepWalk outperforms other latent representation methods for creating social dimensions , especially when labeled nodes are scarce.
DeepWalk achieves all of that while being an online algorithm that is trivially parallelizable.
DeepWalk achieves all of that while being an online algorithm that is trivially parallelizable.
Our contributions are as follows: • We introduce deep learning as a tool to analyze graphs, to build robust representations that are suitable for statistical modeling.
DeepWalk learns structural regularities present within short random walks. • We extensively evaluate our representations on multilabel classification tasks on several social networks.
We show significantly increased classification performance in the presence of label sparsity, getting improvements 5%-10% of Micro F1, on the sparsest problems we consider.

In some cases, DeepWalk's representations can outperform its competitors even when given 60% less training data. • We demonstrate the scalability of our algorithm by building representations of web-scale graphs, (such as YouTube) using a parallel implementation.
